metadata.career_relevance: Spring Boot testing is mandatory in any professional team. @WebMvcTest lets you test HTTP controllers in milliseconds without a database. @DataJpaTest validates your queries against a real (H2) database. @SpringBootTest runs full integration tests. Knowing which test slice to use and when is what separates a competent Spring Boot developer from an expert. Every PR at Flipkart, Razorpay, and Zerodha requires tests to merge.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll write @WebMvcTest tests for controllers using MockMvc, @DataJpaTest tests for repositories with @Import for services, @SpringBootTest full integration tests with TestRestTemplate, and understand which slice to use for each testing need.

concept_explanation.which_slice_when:
**Choosing the right test slice:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ What are you testing?          │ Use                        │
├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
│ HTTP layer: routing, status    │ @WebMvcTest                │
│ codes, JSON, validation        │ (fast: ~1 sec)             │
├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
│ Database queries, derived      │ @DataJpaTest               │
│ methods, @Query                │ (fast: ~2 sec)             │
├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
│ Business logic only            │ Plain JUnit + Mockito      │
│ (no Spring context needed)     │ (fastest: ~0.1 sec)        │
├────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
│ Full stack: controller         │ @SpringBootTest RANDOM_PORT│
│ through to database            │ (slow: ~5-10 sec, use less)│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rule of thumb:
- Most tests: @WebMvcTest or @DataJpaTest (fast slices)
- Few tests: @SpringBootTest (critical paths only)
- Always: plain JUnit for pure business logic
```

visual_aid.description: Three-column diagram: (1) @WebMvcTest — shows the HTTP request → MockMvc → Controller → @MockBean Service (returns stub). Arrow says 'No DB, no full context'. Loads: Controller, ControllerAdvice, Jackson. Mocked: Service, Repository. (2) @DataJpaTest — shows Service → Repository → H2 Database. @Import(Service.class) shown as dotted line. No HTTP. Rollback arrow shows each test gets clean state. (3) @SpringBootTest RANDOM_PORT — full stack: TestRestTemplate sends HTTP → real Tomcat → Controller → Service → Repository → H2. Label: 'slowest but most realistic'. Below: timing comparison bar chart: @WebMvcTest ~500ms, @DataJpaTest ~2s, @SpringBootTest ~5-10s.
visual_aid.alt_text: Three Spring Boot test slices: WebMvcTest (HTTP only), DataJpaTest (DB only), SpringBootTest (full stack) with timing comparison

worked_example.scenario: Write a complete three-layer test suite for the Product API from Module 13. One @WebMvcTest for the controller, one @DataJpaTest for the repository, and one @SpringBootTest integration test — each testing only what's relevant to that slice.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Write tests for the complete Product API:
1. @WebMvcTest: GET all, GET/{id} found/not-found, POST valid/invalid
2. @DataJpaTest: save, findByCategory, findByPriceLessThan, duplicate name
3. @SpringBootTest: full CRUD lifecycle end-to-end
worked_example.expected_output:
@WebMvcTest results:
✓ getAll_returns200AndList
✓ getById_found_returns200
✓ getById_notFound_returns404
✓ create_validRequest_returns201
✓ create_blankName_returns400WithFieldError

@DataJpaTest results:
✓ findByCategory_electronics_returns2
✓ create_duplicate_throwsDuplicate
✓ findById_afterSave_returnsProduct
✓ delete_removesProduct

@SpringBootTest result:
✓ fullCrudLifecycle

10 tests passed, 0 failed
Total time: ~6 seconds
things_to_notice: @WebMvcTest runs in ~500ms because it loads only the controller layer. No Spring context startup, no database initialization. The service is a @MockBean — Spring creates a Mockito mock and injects it into the controller.

MockMvc.perform() builds and executes a mock HTTP request. The method-chained andExpect() calls assert on the response. jsonPath('$.name').value('Mouse') uses JSONPath to navigate the response JSON — '$' is the root, '$.length()' gives array size.

@DataJpaTest with @BeforeEach seed: each test gets a rollback transaction. seed() runs before each test, inserts data, and after the test the entire transaction rolls back. The next test starts with an empty database.

@Import(ProductService.class) in @DataJpaTest: the DataJpa slice doesn't load @Service beans automatically. You must explicitly import each service you need. Without it, @Autowired ProductService fails.

@SpringBootTest with repo.deleteAll() in @BeforeEach: integration tests hit a real database that persists between tests. You must clean up manually — unlike @DataJpaTest which rolls back automatically.

TestRestTemplate vs RestTemplate: TestRestTemplate is configured for test use — it doesn't throw on 4xx/5xx responses, making it easy to assert error status codes. Regular RestTemplate throws HttpClientErrorException on 4xx.

industry_examples.use_case: Every PR triggers a GitHub Actions pipeline: @WebMvcTest tests (fast, run every commit), @DataJpaTest tests (medium, run every commit), @SpringBootTest integration tests (slow, run nightly or on main). This tiered strategy keeps the commit loop fast (< 2 minutes for sliced tests) while still catching integration bugs. The rule: 70% unit/@WebMvcTest, 20% @DataJpaTest, 10% @SpringBootTest.

PaymentController is tested with @WebMvcTest: valid payment 201, invalid amount 400, unknown order 404, insufficient funds 422 — all using @MockBean PaymentService. PaymentRepository is tested with @DataJpaTest: findByOrderIdAndStatus, sumAmountByUser (aggregate @Query). A critical end-to-end test with @SpringBootTest verifies the full payment creation → verification flow against an in-memory H2.

Spring Boot's own test documentation recommends the slice approach. The official Spring Boot guides show exactly the three-layer testing strategy taught here. Test slices (autoconfiguration slices) were introduced specifically to solve the problem of slow, monolithic @SpringBootTest tests. This is the Spring team's own recommended testing strategy.

applications.interview_relevance:
Spring Boot testing interview questions:

- **'What is @WebMvcTest?'** → Loads only the web layer (controller, ControllerAdvice, Jackson). Uses @MockBean for service dependencies. Uses MockMvc for HTTP assertions.
- **'When would you use @SpringBootTest vs @WebMvcTest?'** → @WebMvcTest for unit/controller tests (fast). @SpringBootTest for integration tests (full stack). @WebMvcTest is preferred for most tests.
- **'What is MockMvc?'** → A test client for the Spring MVC layer. `mvc.perform(get('/api/x')).andExpect(status().isOk())` tests HTTP without a real server.
- **'How do you test a Spring Data repository?'** → @DataJpaTest with H2 in-memory. Each test is transactional and rolls back. Add @Import for service classes.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily Spring Boot testing workflow:

- New controller method → @WebMvcTest with @MockBean service
- New repository method → @DataJpaTest with @BeforeEach seed
- Critical user flow → @SpringBootTest RANDOM_PORT
- Pure business logic → plain JUnit + Mockito (no Spring needed)
- CI: run @WebMvcTest + @DataJpaTest on every commit; @SpringBootTest nightly

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Forgetting @MockBean for service dependencies in @WebMvcTest — Spring can't find the service bean and throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException.
common_gaps.remediation: @WebMvcTest doesn't load @Service beans. Every dependency of the controller must be declared as @MockBean in the test class. Check the controller constructor — each injected dependency needs a @MockBean.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: NoSuchBeanDefinitionException or UnsatisfiedDependencyException at test startup

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: @SpringBootTest tests that create data affect each other — test order dependency.
common_gaps.remediation: @DataJpaTest rolls back automatically. @SpringBootTest does NOT. Use @BeforeEach to clean: `repo.deleteAll()`. Or use `@Transactional` on the test class (rolls back each test). Or use an H2 in-memory database that resets per test run.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Tests pass in isolation but fail when run together

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using @SpringBootTest with RANDOM_PORT but trying to use MockMvc — doesn't work.
common_gaps.remediation: MockMvc works with MOCK environment (default). TestRestTemplate works with RANDOM_PORT. For RANDOM_PORT: `@Autowired TestRestTemplate rest`. For MockMvc: use `@SpringBootTest` without environment (default is MOCK) or use `@AutoConfigureMockMvc` with RANDOM_PORT.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: TestRestTemplate connection refused on port 0; MockMvc not working with real port

key_takeaways: @WebMvcTest(Controller.class): loads only the HTTP layer — fast, focused
@MockBean: creates Mockito mock for a Spring bean — use in @WebMvcTest for all service deps
jsonPath('$.field').value(x): assert JSON response field value
@DataJpaTest: loads JPA + H2 only — each test rolls back automatically
TestRestTemplate: HTTP client for @SpringBootTest — doesn't throw on 4xx/5xx
@BeforeEach repo.deleteAll() in @SpringBootTest — manual cleanup (no auto rollback)

self_check_questions.question: Your @WebMvcTest starts but fails with 'No qualifying bean of type ProductService'. What's wrong?
self_check_questions.answer: @WebMvcTest does NOT load @Service beans. You declared the controller and the test, but ProductService is not in the test context. Fix: add `@MockBean ProductService service;` to the test class. @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer — every service the controller depends on must be explicitly mocked with @MockBean.

self_check_questions.question: You're testing a repository query `findByStatus('PENDING')`. Should you use @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, or @SpringBootTest?
self_check_questions.answer: @DataJpaTest is the right choice. It loads JPA and H2, creates the schema, and gives you the actual repository bean. @WebMvcTest doesn't load repositories. @SpringBootTest would work but is overkill — it loads the full context when you only need to test the database query. In @DataJpaTest: seed data with repo.save() in @BeforeEach, call repo.findByStatus('PENDING'), assert.

self_check_questions.question: Two @SpringBootTest tests run together. The first creates a product named 'Mouse'. The second tests that no products exist initially. The second fails. Why and how do you fix it?
self_check_questions.answer: Unlike @DataJpaTest, @SpringBootTest does NOT roll back between tests. The product created in test 1 persists in the H2 database for test 2. Fix options: (1) @BeforeEach repo.deleteAll() — clean before each test. (2) @Transactional on the test class — rolls back each test (usually works but can hide transaction issues). (3) Isolation: design tests to not depend on 'no data' — count before and after instead. Option 1 is most explicit and reliable.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 13: Spring Boot Fundamentals is complete! You now have the full professional Spring Boot skill set: application setup, REST controllers, JPA persistence, validation, exception handling, and testing. From here, the course moves into specialized domains: Module 14 covers advanced Spring Boot patterns (configuration properties, caching, async), or specialized domains like Microservices, Security, or React frontend. Every module from here applies Spring Boot — you've built the foundation everything else needs.
